content:
# Leviticus Chapter 18 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68v–69r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_018_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_018_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 104–105

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: I am the LORD your God.

**3** You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived, and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan, to which I am bringing you. You shall not walk in their statutes.

**4** You shall observe my rules and keep my statutes and walk in them. I am the LORD your God.

**5** You shall therefore keep my statutes and my rules; if a person does them, he shall live by them. I am the LORD.

**6** "None of you shall approach any one of his close relatives to uncover nakedness. I am the LORD.

**7** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father or the nakedness of your mother; she is your mother, you shall not uncover her nakedness.

**8** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife; it is your father's nakedness.

**9** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your sister, the daughter of your father or the daughter of your mother, whether born at home or born abroad.

**10** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your son's daughter or your daughter's daughter, for their nakedness is your own nakedness.

**11** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife's daughter, fathered by your father; she is your sister, you shall not uncover her nakedness.

**12** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's sister; she is your father's close relative.

**13** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister, for she is your mother's close relative.

**14** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's brother: you shall not approach his wife; she is your aunt.

**15** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your daughter-in-law; she is your son's wife, you shall not uncover her nakedness.

**16** You shall not uncover the nakedness of your brother's wife; it is your brother's nakedness.

**17** You shall not uncover the nakedness of a woman and her daughter, and you shall not take her son's daughter or her daughter's daughter to uncover her nakedness; they are close relatives — it is depravity.

**18** And you shall not take a woman as a rival to her sister, uncovering her nakedness while her sister is still alive.

**19** "You shall not approach a woman to uncover her nakedness while she is in her menstrual impurity.

**20** And you shall not lie sexually with your neighbor's wife and so make yourself unclean with her.

**21** You shall not give any of your children to pass them through to Molech, and so profane the name of your God. I am the LORD.

**22** You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination.

**23** And you shall not lie with any animal and so make yourself unclean with it, nor shall any woman give herself to an animal to lie with it; it is depravity.

**24** "Do not make yourselves unclean by any of these things, for by all these things the nations I am driving out before you have made themselves unclean,

**25** and the land became unclean, so that I punished its iniquity, and the land vomited out its inhabitants.

**26** But you shall keep my statutes and my rules and do none of these abominations, either the native or the sojourner who sojourns among you —

**27** for all these abominations the people of the land did, who were before you, so that the land became unclean —

**28** lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean, as it vomited out the nation that was before you.

**29** For everyone who does any of these abominations — the persons who do them shall be cut off from among their people.

**30** So keep my charge never to do any of these abominable customs that were practiced before you, so that you do not make yourselves unclean by them. I am the LORD your God."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68v–69r:**
Leviticus 18 occupies the latter portion of folio 68v and the opening of folio 69r in the Leningrad Codex. The chapter follows immediately upon the blood-holiness legislation of chapter 17, completing the opening unit of the Holiness Code (H source, chs. 17–26). The Masoretic text is well preserved at this point; the three-column layout of B19A shows no significant lacunae or correction marks in this chapter. The Masorah parva in the margins notes ketiv-qere distinctions at several points in the list of prohibitions. The chapter is unified by a distinctive literary structure: an opening frame (vv. 1–5) giving the theological rationale, a central catalogue of prohibited sexual unions (vv. 6–23) organized by degrees of kinship and types of transgression, and a closing frame (vv. 24–30) applying the cosmic-moral consequences. The repeated formula "I am the LORD your God" / "I am the LORD" (vv. 2, 4, 5, 6, 21, 30) constitutes the rhetorical spine of the chapter — YHWH's self-identification as the ground and authority of the prohibitions.

The chapter has long been recognized as a discrete literary unit with strong compositional coherence. The legal catalogue (vv. 6–23) employs the formula lo tegaleh ervat X ("you shall not uncover the nakedness of X") as its refrain, creating a rhythmic list that functions mnemonically. The term ervah (nakedness) serves as a covering euphemism for sexual relations throughout. The chapter's placement of the Molech prohibition (v. 21) and the bestiality prohibition (v. 23) within a primarily kinship-oriented catalogue is significant: the Holiness Code treats all three categories — incest, child sacrifice to Molech, same-sex relations, and bestiality — as violations of the same fundamental holiness principle, not simply as distinct offences.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 104–105:**
Leviticus 18 in the Vaticanus spans pages 104 and 105, continuing from the chapter division visible on page 104 (marked with the LXX's chapter numeration). The LXX of Leviticus 18 follows the MT very closely in structure, with the same opening frame, central catalogue, and closing frame. The LXX renders ervah consistently as ἀσχημοσύνη (aschēmosynē, "indecency, shameful/improper thing"), which is the standard LXX equivalent for the Hebrew term. Notable variants appear at v. 5 (the va-chai bahem formula), v. 18 (rival-wife prohibition), and v. 21 (the Molech verse), where the LXX offers significant alternate readings. The LXX uses the term βδέλυγμα (bdelygma, "abomination, abhorrence") for both teva'ah (vv. 22, 26, 27, 29) and zimmah (v. 17) — partially conflating two distinct Hebrew terms. The LXX's rendering of v. 25 (the land-vomiting verse) is structurally modified compared to the MT.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "I am the LORD your God" (vv. 2, 4, 6, 30) — ani YHWH eloheikhem / ani YHWH:**
The self-identification formula ani YHWH ("I am the LORD") and ani YHWH eloheikhem ("I am the LORD your God") functions as the constitutive ground for each block of legislation. It is not merely a signature; it is a covenantal assertion that makes the command binding. The shortened form ani YHWH ("I am the LORD") appears at vv. 5, 6, 21 as a mid-catalogue assertion of authority. These formulae are rendered with the conventional small-caps "LORD" for YHWH throughout. The formula frames the chapter: it opens (v. 2) and closes (v. 30) the entire unit, creating an inclusio that places all the prohibitions within the space of YHWH's sovereign identity.

**2. "He shall live by them" (v. 5) — va-chai bahem:**
Verse 5 is among the most exegetically consequential sentences in the entire Torah: asher ya'aseh otam ha-adam va-chai bahem ("which, if a person does them, he shall live by them"). The verb chai (Qal perfect, "he shall live") with the preposition bahem ("by/through them") presents obedience as the path to life. The rendering "he shall live by them" is preferred to "he will live in them" or "he may live by them." The formal equivalence tradition (ESV, NASB) renders va-chai as "he shall live" with the preposition rendered "by." The verse is cited by Paul in Galatians 3:12 and Romans 10:5 as a foil against the sufficiency of Torah-obedience for justification; Paul reads it as showing that the Torah-obedience path demands comprehensive performance. The LXX rendering (καὶ ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς, "and he shall live in/by them") uses ἐν αὐτοῖς ("in them" rather than "by them"), which may slightly alter the sense toward participation rather than instrumentality — though the Greek preposition ἐν can carry both senses. The LOGOS rendering "he shall live by them" follows the ESV/NASB pattern and preserves the instrumental-means nuance of the Hebrew bahem.

**3. "Uncover nakedness" (vv. 6–19) — le-gallot ervah:**
The Hebrew idiom gallah ervah (Pi'el of galah, "to uncover/reveal," + ervah, "nakedness") is a consistent Levitical euphemism for sexual intercourse in a forbidden relational context. The phrase appears more than twenty times in this chapter alone. The rendering "uncover nakedness" is retained throughout as the formal-equivalent rendering, following KJV/ESV/NASB tradition. It is preferable to "have sexual relations with" (NIV) or "have intercourse with" because the Hebrew idiom is itself a euphemism that should be preserved as such — the metaphor of "uncovering" nakedness implies exposure, violation of what is meant to remain veiled, and carries a connotation of shame that the more clinical modern renderings lose. The LXX ἀποκαλύψεις τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην ("you shall uncover the indecency/shameful thing") reproduces the idiom exactly.

**4. "Close relative" (vv. 6, 12, 13, 17) — she'er besaro:**
The Hebrew phrase she'er besaro (literally "flesh of his flesh" or "the flesh of his body") denotes a near blood-relative, close kin. The term she'er (related to "flesh, close relative, close connection") functions as a kinship term for the innermost circle of family. It is rendered "close relative" throughout (vv. 6, 12, 13) following ESV/NASB usage. The rendering "close of kin" (NRSV) and "near of kin" (KJV) are comparable alternatives; "close relative" is preferred for clarity. The LXX renders she'er besaro with οἰκεῖος τῆς σαρκὸς αὐτοῦ ("one who is of his own flesh/household") in v. 6, and uses συγγενής ("kinsman/relative") in the related lists, showing the same semantic field.

**5. "Depravity" (vv. 17, 23) — zimmah:**
The Hebrew term zimmah (from zamam, "to plan, devise") denotes a particularly vile or morally revolting act — a "devising" or "scheming" that is infamous and abhorrent. It appears at v. 17 (the prohibition on taking a woman and her daughter) and implicitly at v. 23 (bestiality, described as tevel). For v. 17, the term zimmah is rendered "depravity" — stronger than "wickedness" (KJV) but more precisely connoting calculated moral debasement than the general "perversion" (NIV). The LXX renders zimmah as ἀσέβεια ("impiety/ungodliness") at v. 17, which is an interpretive choice that shifts the category from social-moral offense to theological offense against God.

**6. "Abomination" (vv. 22, 26, 27, 29, 30) — teva'ah:**
The Hebrew teva'ah (often spelled to'evah) is one of the most significant evaluative terms in the Hebrew Bible. It denotes something that causes revulsion, something ritually/morally loathsome — often specifically in the context of things abhorrent to YHWH. It is the standard term applied in Leviticus, Deuteronomy, and the Prophets to violations that contaminate the land and bring divine judgment. The rendering "abomination" follows the ESV/KJV/NASB tradition and is retained throughout this chapter. The LXX consistently renders teva'ah with βδέλυγμα (bdelygma), the standard Greek term for ritual/moral revulsion. At v. 22, the abomination label is applied specifically to male homosexual intercourse: teva'ah hi ("it is an abomination"). The LOGOS translation renders this without attenuation — "it is an abomination" — following the formal equivalence commitment to render the evaluative vocabulary of the source text.

**7. "Pass them through to Molech" (v. 21) — le-ha'avir la-Molekh:**
The prohibition at v. 21 reads: u-mi-zar'akha lo-titen le-ha'avir la-Molekh — "You shall not give any of your children to pass them through to Molech." The verb ha'avir (Hiphil of avar, "to cause to pass over/through") is the technical term for the practice of child sacrifice or dedication associated with Molech. Scholarly debate exists as to whether ha'avir la-Molekh means literal burning/killing of children or a dedication rite (such as passing through fire as a consecration, without death). The archaeological and textual evidence (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 32:35; Ezek 16:21; 20:26, 31) strongly supports the interpretation that live children were burned in fire as sacrifices. The rendering "to pass them through to Molech" preserves the technical idiom without forcing the question of the exact mechanism; the verb ha'avir is kept as "pass through" rather than "sacrifice to" (which over-specifies) or "devote to" (which under-specifies). The addition "to pass them through" (rather than just "give to Molech") follows the exact Hebrew phrase and preserves the ritualistic specificity. The verse concludes: "and so profane the name of your God" — ve-lo techalel et shem elohekha — "you shall not profane the name of your God." The verb chalal (Pi'el, "to profane/defile/desecrate") applied to God's name (shem) indicates that child sacrifice is not merely a social crime but a direct desecration of the holy name of YHWH.

The LXX renders v. 21 as: ἀπὸ τοῦ σπέρματός σου οὐ δώσεις λατρεύειν ἄρχοντι ("from your seed you shall not give to serve/worship the ruler"). This is a highly significant LXX variant: Vaticanus reads ἄρχοντι ("ruler, lord") rather than Μολόχ (Moloch). This appears to be an early Jewish translation strategy that reinterprets or avoids the divine name Molech, rendering it as a common noun ("the ruler/lord" — possibly a euphemism, or the result of a different vocalization of the Hebrew מֶלֶך melek, "king/ruler," as opposed to the Masoretic vocalization מֹלֶךְ Molekh with the vowels of boshet, "shame"). The MT Molekh form reflects a traditional Masoretic interpretive vocalization designed to convey contempt. The LXX's choice of ἄρχοντι is a significant variant preserved in the notes.

**8. "You shall not lie with a male as with a woman" (v. 22) — ve-et-zachar lo tishkav mishkevei ishah:**
The verse reads: ve-et-zachar lo tishkav mishkevei ishah teva'ah hi. A literal rendering: "And with a male you shall not lie lyings of a woman; it is an abomination." The rendering "You shall not lie with a male as with a woman; it is an abomination" is the standard formal equivalence translation (ESV, NASB, KJV). The phrase mishkevei ishah ("lyings of a woman") is a construct plural of mishkav ("lying, a lying down") — the plural form is idiomatic for "the ways/modes of lying with a woman" and indicates the sexual act framed by comparison to heterosexual intercourse. The LOGOS translation preserves this with "as with a woman" rather than the periphrastic "homosexual acts" (NIV) or similar dynamic equivalents. The evaluative term teva'ah hi is rendered "it is an abomination" — the formal-equivalent tradition follows the MT's categorical statement without attenuation.

**9. "Depravity" (v. 23) — tevel:**
The term tevel (appearing only in Lev 18:23 and 20:12) is distinct from zimmah (v. 17). The root tbl may be related to confusion or mixing; the term appears to denote a violation of created categories — a confusion/mixing of kinds that is deeply disordering. It is applied at v. 23 to both male and female bestiality: "it is depravity." The rendering "depravity" is chosen over KJV "confusion" (which reflects an older etymology) or "perversion" (NIV) to maintain the sense of something profoundly disordered and morally depraved. NASB "perversion" and ESV "perversion" are comparable; "depravity" is preferred as capturing the gravity of the term without over-specifying the etymological sense.

**10. "The land vomited out its inhabitants" (v. 25) — va-taqi ha-aretz et-yoshveiha:**
The imagery of the land "vomiting out" (Qal imperfect of qi, "to vomit") its inhabitants is among the most striking metaphors in the entire Pentateuch. The land is personified as a moral agent with a digestive system: the accumulated wickedness of the Canaanite nations has become so great that the land physically expels them as an organism expels something indigestible. The verb qi is rendered "vomited out" throughout (vv. 25, 28), following ESV, NASB, KJV. The same threat is applied to Israel in v. 28: "lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean." This framing presents the conquest of Canaan not as ethnic expulsion but as moral-cosmic consequence: the land itself is presented as the agent of divine justice, and Israel is placed under the same moral condition that expelled the Canaanites. The LXX renders va-taqi as προσώχθισεν ("was disgusted/loathed") in v. 25, which shifts the metaphor from physical vomiting to emotional revulsion — a significant softening of the MT's visceral image.

**11. "Make yourself unclean" (vv. 20, 23, 24) — ve-nitmeita / lo tittamu:**
The verb tame (Qal/Niphal/Hithpael, "to be/become unclean, to defile") and its cognate nouns form the central ritual-purity category throughout Leviticus. In chapter 18 the term is used in both the prohibitions and the consequences: the prohibited acts make both the individual and the land tameh (unclean). The rendering "make yourself unclean" (Hithpael reflexive, vv. 20, 23) preserves the reflexive force — the defilement acts back upon the agent. "Make yourselves unclean" (v. 24, plural) is rendered accordingly. The LXX uses μιαίνω ("to pollute/defile") and its cognates consistently for tame and its forms.

**12. "Punished its iniquity" (v. 25) — va-efqod avonah:**
The verb paqad (Qal, "to visit, to attend to, to punish") with avonah ("its iniquity") is rendered "I punished its iniquity." The verb paqad al (to "visit upon") is the standard judicial idiom for God's punitive intervention in response to sin. The rendering "punished its iniquity" follows ESV/NASB usage. "I visited upon it its iniquity" (more literal) is more awkward; "I brought its iniquity upon it" (NIV) is a dynamic paraphrase. The formal rendering "I punished its iniquity" maintains the judicial force while being fully clear in English.

**13. "Abominable customs" (v. 30) — chuqqot ha-tova'ot:**
The closing verse introduces the construct phrase chuqqot ha-tova'ot — "the statutes/customs of the abominations" or "abominable customs." This phrase combines chuqqot (plural of chuqqah, "statute, custom, prescribed practice") with the adjectival genitive ha-tova'ot ("the abominations/the abominable"). The rendering "abominable customs" is preferred over "detestable customs" (NIV) or "abominable practices" (ESV) — the word "customs" for chuqqot maintains the ironic resonance of the opening frame (vv. 3–4), where YHWH commanded Israel not to walk in the chuqqot (statutes/customs) of Egypt and Canaan. The closing verse uses the same word (chuqqot) to label those Canaanite practices as "abominable customs," creating a deliberate inclusio with the chapter's opening.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| 5 | va-chai bahem ("he shall live by them") | καὶ ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς | LXX: ἐν αὐτοῖς = "in them" rather than "by them"; preposition ἐν vs. Hebrew bet; Paul cites this verse in Gal 3:12 and Rom 10:5 |
| 6 | she'er besaro ("close relative") | οἰκεῖος τῆς σαρκὸς αὐτοῦ | LXX: "one of the household of his flesh"; renders she'er with οἰκεῖος (household member) |
| 17 | zimmah hi ("it is depravity") | ἀσέβειά ἐστιν | LXX: ἀσέβεια = "impiety/ungodliness" rather than social-moral depravity; shifts category from moral to theological |
| 18 | ve-lo tiqach ishah el achoteha ("you shall not take a woman as a rival to her sister") | καὶ γυναῖκα ἐπ᾽ ἀδελφῇ αὐτῆς οὐ λήμψῃ | LXX: close equivalence; ἐπ᾽ ἀδελφῇ = "upon/over her sister" — same relational sense |
| 21 | le-ha'avir la-Molekh ("to pass through to Molech") | λατρεύειν ἄρχοντι ("to serve/worship the ruler") | Significant variant: LXX avoids the divine name Molekh, rendering as ἄρχων ("ruler"); likely reflects alternate vocalization of melek ("king") rather than the Masoretic Molekh; early Jewish avoidance of the name |
| 21 | ve-lo techalel et shem elohekha ("you shall not profane the name of your God") | καὶ οὐ βεβηλώσεις τὸ ὄνομα τὸ ἅγιον ("you shall not profane the holy name") | LXX adds τὸ ἅγιον ("the holy"), making explicit what is implied in MT |
| 22 | mishkevei ishah ("as one lies with a woman") | κοίτην γυναικός ("the bed/lying of a woman") | LXX: κοίτη = "bed/sexual lying"; exact functional equivalent |
| 22 | teva'ah hi ("it is an abomination") | βδέλυγμά ἐστιν | LXX: βδέλυγμα = standard Greek equivalent for teva'ah; exact equivalence |
| 23 | tevel hi ("it is depravity") | παράνομον γάρ ἐστιν | LXX: "for it is unlawful/transgressing" — παράνομος = "contrary to law"; very different from MT's tevel ("depravity/disorder of kinds"); LXX moralizes where MT speaks of category-violation |
| 25 | va-taqi ha-aretz et-yoshveiha ("and the land vomited out its inhabitants") | καὶ προσώχθισεν ἡ γῆ τοὺς κατοικοῦντας αὐτήν | LXX: προσώχθισεν = "was disgusted/loathed"; significantly softer than MT's physical vomiting metaphor; same softening at v. 28 |
| 25 | va-efqod avonah ("I punished its iniquity") | καὶ ἀνταπέδωκα ἀδικίαν αὐτῇ | LXX: ἀνταπέδωκα = "I repaid/rendered back its injustice"; ἀδικία ("injustice/wrongdoing") for avon ("iniquity") |
| 28 | va-taqi ha-aretz etchem ("lest the land vomit you out") | καὶ προσοχθίσῃ ὑμῖν ἡ γῆ | LXX: "and the land be disgusted with you"; same softening of vomiting metaphor as v. 25 |
| 30 | chuqqot ha-tova'ot ("abominable customs") | τῶν νομίμων τῶν βδελυγμάτων τούτων | LXX: "the ordinances of these abominations"; νόμιμον for chuqqot; βδελύγματα for tova'ot |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings

| Hebrew Term | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|-------------|-----------------|-------|
| YHWH | the LORD | Small capitals as conventional |
| ani YHWH | I am the LORD | Self-identification formula; constitutive ground of commands |
| ani YHWH eloheikhem | I am the LORD your God | Full form at vv. 2, 4, 30 |
| ervah | nakedness | Euphemistic idiom throughout; preserved as formal equivalent |
| gallah ervah | uncover nakedness | Idiomatic euphemism for prohibited sexual intercourse |
| she'er besaro | close relative | Kinship term; lit. "flesh of his flesh" |
| teva'ah | abomination | Standard formal rendering; LXX: βδέλυγμα |
| zimmah | depravity | v. 17; stronger than "wickedness"; deliberate vile act |
| tevel | depravity | v. 23; category-violation; disorder of kinds |
| ha'avir la-Molekh | pass through to Molech | Technical term for child sacrifice ritual; preserved intact |
| Molekh | Molech | MT vocalization retained; see note 7 |
| va-chai bahem | he shall live by them | v. 5; instrumental bahem rendered "by them" |
| va-taqi ha-aretz | the land vomited out | Physical vomiting metaphor preserved; MT more visceral than LXX |
| tame / nitmeita | unclean / make yourself unclean | Ritual purity terminology consistent with Lev 1–17 |
| chuqqah | statute / statutes | Consistent with opening frame vv. 3–4 and closing v. 30 |
| mishpat | rule(s) | v. 4, 5: legal ordinances; cf. chuqqah (statute) |
| paqad avon | punish iniquity | v. 25: judicial visitation of God upon sin |
| chalal | profane | Pi'el: desecrate the holy name |
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Formal throughout |
| ha-ger ha-gar | the sojourner who sojourns | Cognate verbal construction preserved; v. 26 |
| ha-ezrach | the native | v. 26: native-born Israelite |
| avon | iniquity | v. 25; distinguished from chatah (sin) |

---

— LOGOS
